Motivation on the Geometric Satake Isomorphism ​

Lecture 2 - March 10, 2026Speaker: Yuchan Lee Location: Room 104

Notation and Preliminaries ​

  • G: split connected reductive group over F.
  • F: non-archimedean local field, with O=OF, t=π, and k=F/O with q=|k|.
  • Fix T⊂B⊂G, W:=NG(T)/CG(T)=NG(T)/T, the Weyl group.
  • Φ=Φ+⊔Φ− by T⊂B, and Δ⊂Φ+.
  • Dominant coweight X∗(T)+:={μ∈X∗(T)∣⟨μ,α⟩≥0∀α∈Δ}, and ρ=12∑α∈Φ+α.

We have several well-known facts:

Lemma

Lemma (Cartan Decomposition)

G(F)=⨆μ∈X∗(T)+G(O)μ(t)G(O)

Motivation ​

Remark

Aim: motivation of the geometric Satake equivalence, and its ingredients.

Recall that the classical Satake isomorphism is

Theorem

Theorem (Classical Satake Isomorphism)

S:H(G(F),G(O))≅C[X∗(T)]W≅K0(RepG^)

Then, over the isomorphism, we have two natural bases of the isomorphism.

Lemma

Lemma (Two bases)

  1. A basis of H(G(F),G(O)) is

    {cμ:=1G(O)μ(t)G(O)}μ∈X∗(T)+

  2. A basis of C[X∗(T)]W is

    {φμ:=q⟨ρ,μ⟩tr(Vμ)}μ∈X∗(T)+

    where Vμ is the irreducible representation of G^ whose highest weight vector is μ.

This has a comparison.

Lemma

Lemma (Kazhdan-Lusztig Polynomial)

We have

S−1φμ=cμ+∑λ≤μdμλ(q)cλ

where dμλ(x) is the Kazhdan-Lusztig polynomial for the affine Weyl group of G^.

Surprisingly, each coefficient of dμλ(x) is positive.

We can classically prove the things, but we want to prove and understand the following things:

  1. Semi-simpleness of RepG^.
  2. Positivity of the coefficients of dμλ(x).
  3. Natural appearance of G^.
  4. Generalization into the Iwahori Level.

Remark

The Geometric Satake Equivalence can explain the four questions.

Then, we need to geometrize it. The geometrization is the goal of the lecture.

Classical ObjectGeometric Object
G(F)/G(O)Affine Grassmannian
Cartan Decomposition: G(O)-orbit of G(F)/G(O)Schubert Stratification: L+G-orbit in GrG
H(G(F),G(O))PervL+G(GrG)

Affine Grassmannian ​

We set k=Fq, with F=Fq((t)) and O=Fq[[t]].

  1. Define Affk to be the category of affine k-schemes, which is the same as the opposite category of k-algebras.
  2. Endow Affk with the fpqc topology. In other words, for each affine k-scheme X, there is a covering {Ui→X} such that ∐Ui→X is flat and surjective.
  3. Zhu's notation: category of sheaves on Affk is Spk (also said to be k-space), and category of schemes is Schk.

Definition

Definition (Ind-Scheme)

An ind-scheme over k is a k-space that can be written as a filtered direct limit

X=lim→i∈I⁡Xi

such that Xi is a scheme and for every arrow i→j in I, the corresponding Xi→Xj is a closed embedding.

Then, an ind-scheme X over k is called ind-affine/projective/of finite type/etc., if there is a representation of X such that each Xi has the structure.

Remark

Not restricted to the affine scheme for each Xi, but regarding X as a sheaf, this must be a sheaf defined over all affine schemes over k.

In addition, in terms of points (R-points), we can regard the ind-scheme as a union of Xi(R) along with the relation made by I.

Affine Grassmannian of the general linear group ​

Definition

Definition (Affine Grassmannian) (restricted to GLn case)

For a k-algebra R, an R-family of lattices in k((t))n is a finitely generated projective R[[t]]-submodule Λ of R((t))n such that

Λ⊗R[[t]]R((t))≅R((t))n

Then, an affine Grassmannian GrGLn is a (pre)sheaf (FACT: this is indeed a sheaf)

Affk→SetsR↦{the set of R-families of lattices in k((t))n}

This means, especially

GrGLn(k)={finitely generated projective k[[t]]-modules Λ⊂k((t))n such that Λ⊗k[[t]]k((t))≅k((t))n}

Note that projective is free in this case.

In addition, take an element Λ0=k[[t]]n then the action GLn(F)×GrGLn(k)→GrGLn(k) by g↦gΛ0 is transitive, with the stabilizer

StabGLn(F)(Λ0)=Autk[[t]](k[[t]]n)=GLn(O)

This means

GrGLn(k)≅GLn(F)/GLn(O)

Quotient structure ​

Recall that R⊗kk[[t]]=R[[t]] and R⊗kk((t))=R((t)), so we can consider the following objects as (pre)sheaves over k.

Definition

Definition (Jet/Arc/Positive Loop and Loop group)

For a presheaf X over O (resp. over F), the jet = arc = positive loop group L+X of X is the presheaf over k

L+X:R↦X(R⊗kk[[t]])=X(R[[t]])

Likewise, the loop group LX of X is the presheaf over k

LX:R↦X(R((t)))

Proposition

Proposition (Properties of the Jet and the Loop group)

  1. Let X be a scheme of finite type over O, then L+X is represented by a scheme over k.
  2. If X is an affine scheme over k, then L+X is an affine scheme over k.
  3. Let X be an affine scheme of finite type over F, then LX is represented by an ind-affine scheme over k. (NOT A SCHEME IN GENERAL)
  4. If X― is affine and of finite type scheme over O and X―×OF≅F, then L+X⊂LX is a closed subscheme. In other words, there is a filtration LX=lim→i∈I⁡(LX)i of schemes (LX)i such that L+X⊂(LX)i is a closed embedding for each i.

Since we know the representability, it suffices for us to use the R-points of it.

Application to the general linear group ​

Let us consider GLn as an affine scheme over O.

  • G:=GLn over O.
  • LG:=GLn×OF.

Then, there is a natural action

LG×GrG→GrG(g,Λ)↦gΛ

for each R-point. (Note that in this case, the affine Grassmannian is a set of lattices, so the general affine Grassmannian comes from this idea.)

Proposition

The affine Grassmannian GrG is identified with the fpqc quotient (LG/L+G).

Affine Grassmannian of the general reductive group ​

Definition

For a smooth affine k-group G, the affine Grassmannian GrG is the fpqc quotient [LG/L+G].

Proposition

GrG is represented by an ind-scheme, ind-of finite type over k.

If G is reductive, then GrG is an ind-projective scheme.

Schubert Stratification ​

In this section, let k be an algebraically closed field with finite characteristic.

Remark

Motivation: make a geometrization of the Cartan decomposition, stratified by X∗(T)+, the dominant coweights.

Definition

For μ∈X∗(T)+, μ(t)∈GrG(k). (In general, t∈R[[t]] so μ(t)∈GrG(R).) Abusing the notation to [μ(t)]∈GrG(R).

We define a Schubert cell Grμ as the L+G-orbit of [μ(t)] in GrG.

In other words, the image sheaf of

aμ:L+G→GrGg↦g⋅[μ(t)]

on fpqc site.

Then, a Schubert variety is

Gr≤μ:=⋃λ≤μGrλ

See the original paper of Zhu - chapter 2, using "inv", to define the k-points of the above at first, and making the reduced induced scheme structure on it. Thus, this is the reason for using the algebraically closed field.

Proposition

Proposition (Representability)

  1. Grμ is a smooth quasi-projective variety of dimension (2ρ,μ).
  2. Gr≤μ is a projective variety, and it is a Zariski closure of Grμ. (Not in general, smooth)

Note that Gr≤μ(k)=⨆λ≤μGrλ(k) for an algebraically closed field k, but in general, this is NOT a disjoint union as a scheme.

We use the Schubert variety instead of the Schubert cell, because the former is a projective variety, whereas the latter is not. However, in exchange, we lose the smoothness condition, so stratification is needed.

Example

In GLn, we have

Grμ(k)={families of k[[t]]-lattices Λ in k((t))n s.t. ∃g∈GLn(k[[t]]) with gΛ=μ(t)Λ0}

This means

Grμ(k)=G(O)μ(t)G(O)/G(O)

Especially, when G=GL2, with μ=(1,0), then μ(t)=diag(t,1) so we have

Grμ(k)={(ta01)∣a∈k}∪{(100t)}≅P1(k)

In addition, λ≤μ implies λ=μ in (1,0) case, so

Gr≤(1,0)=Gr(1,0)≅P1(k)

For μ=(2,0), the set of dominant coweights λ such that λ≤μ is λ=(2,0),(1,1) so

Gr≤(2,0)=Gr(2,0)∪Gr(1,1)

Since

Gr(2,0)={(t2a01)∣a∈k+tk}∪{(10at2)∣a∈kt}≅k2∪k

and

Gr(1,1)={(t00t)}={∗}

Thus, presumably, Gr≤(2,0) may be a singular quadratic cone, centered on Gr(1,1).

Remark

From the above example, the Schubert variety is, in general, a singular projective variety.

In general, in the Grμ⊂Gr≤μ case, Grμ is a smooth dense open, so the singular part may occur in the boundary part.

Furthermore, usually Grμ has a simple structure, like the affine space An, so it is an easy way to understand the structure of Gr in general.

I think this stratification, or the Schubert Cell idea, is the reason for defining the jet/loop groups and defining the affine Grassmannian over a finite field k, not the local field. When we are considering them over a finite field, each Schubert cell (or a variety) is a finite set, so the explicit calculation by point counting is available. In addition, like for the cohomology stuff.

Perverse Sheaf - Function-Sheaf Dictionary ​

We have the two variations:

(G(O)-equivariant functions on G(F)/G(O))⟺(L+G-equivariant perverse sheaves on GrG)

The former provides no information at singular points. However, the latter has the information.

[BLACKBOX] The Intersection cohomology sheaf, with its stalk cohomology, acts on the singular point (about the Frobenius action).

Note that

Perv(X)⊂Dcb(X)⊂D(Sh(X))

for X a scheme. In general, this can be generalized to the ind-projective schemes. (See Zhu, Appendix A)

Proposition

PervL+G(GrG,Q―l) is a semisimple Abelian category. (For p∤l)

Its simple object is of the form: for μ∈X∗(T)+, take Grμ↪jGr≤μ↪iGrG, for j an open immersion and i a closed immersion. Then, its intersection cohomology sheaves are the simple objects:

ICμ:=i∗j!∗(Ql―[−(2ρ,μ)])

(Recall that (2ρ,μ) is the dimension of Grμ, and j!∗ is the intermediate extension.)

For an open immersion j, we have two pushforwards: j! and j∗. The former is too small, whereas the latter is too large and contains some trash information. Thus, using the image of the "perverse cohomology" pH(j!f)→pH(j∗f), it may contain the right information on the boundary of the open immersion.

In general, for each f∈PervL+G(GrG,Q―l), we can decompose

f≅⨁λ∈X∗(T)+ICλnλ(f)

for the coefficients described by the purity and the parity properties of ICλ.

Purity and parity ​

Definition

Definition (Purity and Parity)

For an algebraic scheme X over k, let f be a Ql―-sheaf. For a closed point x∈X, the geometric Frobenius σx∈Gal(k―/k(x)) induces action on fx― where x― is a geometric point over x.

Then, purity and parity are given by the following.

  • Fix q1/2∈Q―l.
  • Choose an isomorphism ι:Q―l→∼C such that ι(q1/2)∈R<0.

For a complex K∈Dcb(X):

  1. (ι-pure) We say K satisfies ι-pure of weight k if for any closed point x with the geometric point x― over x, all eigenvalues α of σx:Hx―i(K)→Hx―i(K) satisfy |ι(α)|2=|ι(x)|i+k. Especially, for x∈X(k), |ι(α)|2=qi+k.

  2. (Parity) K has even or odd parity if Hx―i(K)≠0 only if i is even or odd.

Proposition

Simple object ICμ in PervL+G(GrG,Q―l) has

  • pure of weight −(2ρ,μ)
  • even parity

Grothendieck function-sheaf dictionary ​

This is the reason why we can consider the spherical Hecke operators as the perverse sheaves.

Definition

Definition (Dictionary)

We have the map

PervL+G(GrG,Q―l)→{functions on GrG(k)=G(F)/G(O)}

given by

A↦fA:x↦∑i(−1)itr(σx,Hx―i(A))

Then, it has a basic property:

Proposition

A is L+G-equivariant implies Ax=Ay when x and y belong to the same L+G-orbit, which means fA is G(O)-equivariant.

In addition, we have the following basic facts:

Proposition

Proposition (Properties)

  1. For A1 and A2, we havefA1∗A2=fA1∗fA2where the former operation is defined later. (Fusion product)
  2. fICμ∈H(G(F),G(O)), which matches with Vμ∈Rep(G^) under the classical Satake isomorphism, up to sign and q1/2 power.
  3. (Basic FACT) For p∈PervL+G(GrG,Q―l), we have fp[d]=(−1)dfp.

Proof of part 2.

For x=λ(t) (it suffices to consider this case, as fICμ is G(O)-invariant), we want to calculate the value fICμ[(2ρ,μ)](x).

  • λ≰μ: the definition of the intersection cohomology gives that ICμ[(2ρ,μ)] has support only on Gr≤μ, so fICμ[(2ρ,μ)](x)=0.

  • λ=μ: as the intersection cohomology comes from Grμ, we have ICμ[(2ρ,μ)]∣Grλ=Ql― which is a constant sheaf, centered at the level 0, thus fICμ[(2ρ,μ)](x)=tr(σx,Ql―)=1.

  • λ<μ: we know that this is pure with even parity, it suffices to consider the even cohomology, so (see Zhu, Sec 5.6)

    fICμ[(2ρ,μ)](x)=∑itr(σx,Hx―2i(ICμ[(2ρ,μ)]))=∑itr(σx,Hx―2i−(2ρ,μ)(ICμ))=(using the purity)∑iqidim⁡Hx―2i−(2ρ,μ)(ICμ)

  • We know the FACT: dλμ(q)=∑iqidim⁡Hx―2i−(2ρ,μ)(ICμ).

  • Therefore, the function-sheaf dictionary reproduces the Kazhdan-Lusztig polynomials. ◻

Conclusion ​

We described:

  1. Semisimplicity of RepG^: by using the fact that the Perverse sheaf category is a semisimple abelian category.
  2. Positivity of dλμ: comes from the fact that it is described by the sum of the dimensions of the cohomology of the intersection cohomology, and the parity condition ensures only positive terms survive.
  3. Appearance of G^: comes from the Tannakian formalism.
  4. Generalization into the Iwahori-fixed part? — Not covered.

Notes taken from the seminar talk. See Zhu (2016) for the primary reference.
